questions.stem: A payment processor needs to stop unmanaged endpoints from reaching an internal finance application after several policy reminders failed. The service owner is concerned about user impact but accepts compensating controls when justified. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Require device posture checks through NAC before allowing access to the finance network segment.
Publish a stronger acceptable use policy without technical enforcement.
Ask managers to manually approve every endpoint connection by email.
Create a quarterly report showing which users connected from unmanaged devices.

questions.explanation: NAC with posture checking is a technical preventive control that blocks noncompliant devices before access. Monitoring complements it, while policy-only or obscurity-based approaches do not enforce access.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A manufacturing company with OT networks has an unsupported but mission-critical records system that cannot be patched for six months because the vendor is out of business. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. Which TWO responses best address the situation?

choices.text: Disable logging to reduce overhead on the legacy server.
Grant local administrator rights only to senior users.
Expose the service only during business hours and rely on user vigilance.
Document the risk exception, owner approval, and expiration date for the compensating control.
Place the system in a tightly segmented network with allow-listed flows and enhanced monitoring.

questions.explanation: When remediation is not immediately possible, segmentation, allow-listing, and monitoring reduce exposure as compensating controls. A formal exception keeps ownership and review visible.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A municipal government agency found that unauthorized firewall rule changes were made during overnight maintenance. Executives want evidence that the control addresses root cause rather than only the visible symptom. The solution must avoid creating a new single point of failure. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Disable all administrator accounts until the next maintenance cycle.
Increase password complexity for standard users only.
Enable configuration change alerts and compare firewall state against an approved baseline.
Move the firewall logs to cold storage immediately.

questions.explanation: Detective controls identify unauthorized changes, and corrective controls restore the approved state. The other choices do not directly detect or correct the firewall configuration drift.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A logistics company is replacing broad VPN access after users on trusted subnets repeatedly reached systems outside their job duties. The implementation will become part of a standard operating procedure after validation. The team has a small staff and needs a repeatable operational process. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Use a single shared jump-box account for all privileged users.
Trust any device that connects from a corporate IP range.
Disable MFA for users who connect from headquarters.
Deploy policy enforcement points that evaluate identity, device posture, and context for each resource request.

questions.explanation: Zero Trust reduces implicit trust and makes access decisions based on identity, device state, policy, and context. Network location alone should not grant broad access.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 1.2 Fundamental security concepts
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A cloud-native startup needs proof that a specific executive approved high-value wire transfers after several disputes about email-based authorization. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Use a VPN so approvals originate from internal IP space.
Use digital signatures tied to individual certificates for transfer approvals.
Allow assistants to approve transfers from a shared mailbox.
Encrypt all transfer emails with a shared team password.

questions.explanation: Digital signatures provide integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation when tied to individual identities. Immutable logs strengthen evidence. Shared secrets and shared mailboxes weaken attribution.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 1.2 Fundamental security concepts

questions.stem: A legal services company must investigate whether privileged database access followed an approved emergency process. The service owner is concerned about user impact but accepts compensating controls when justified. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. Which TWO responses best address the situation?

choices.text: Verify that the database server had disk encryption enabled.
Rotate all user passwords before reviewing the access record.
Ask the administrator whether the access was appropriate.
Correlate the access record with the approved emergency ticket and session recording.
Review centralized accounting logs that tie the administrator identity, time, command, and target database together.

questions.explanation: Accounting records prove what authenticated and authorized users did. Correlating logs with tickets and recordings supports accountability for privileged actions.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 1.2 Fundamental security concepts

questions.stem: A defense contractor sees outbound connections from a workstation to a file path that should never be opened by normal users. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Delete the honeyfile because it created a false positive.
Replace endpoint protection with a perimeter firewall only.
Investigate the workstation because access to a honeyfile is a high-confidence deception alert.
Assume the event proves data exfiltration completed.

questions.explanation: Honeyfiles and honeytokens are designed to create high-signal alerts when touched. They indicate suspicious activity that warrants investigation, not automatic proof of full exfiltration.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 1.2 Fundamental security concepts

questions.stem: A multinational manufacturer plans to enforce a new deny-by-default egress rule set for production servers. Executives want evidence that the control addresses root cause rather than only the visible symptom. The solution must avoid creating a new single point of failure. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Document the change after implementation if no outage occurs.
Disable all outbound traffic permanently and wait for service owners to complain.
Approve the change only with tested allow-list rules, stakeholder signoff, a maintenance window, and a rollback plan.
Apply the rule immediately because deny-by-default is always safer.

questions.explanation: Security changes can create outages. Proper change management includes testing, ownership, impact analysis, maintenance windows, and a backout plan.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A payment processor wants to restart an authentication service during a certificate update but several legacy applications use undocumented dependencies. The implementation will become part of a standard operating procedure after validation. The team has a small staff and needs a repeatable operational process. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Replace the certificate with a self-signed certificate and skip testing.
Restart the service immediately because certificate updates improve security.
Disable certificate validation in the legacy applications.
Perform impact analysis and dependency mapping before scheduling the certificate change.

questions.explanation: Dependency mapping and impact analysis reduce the risk of security changes breaking legacy applications. Disabling validation or skipping communication creates avoidable risk.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A manufacturing company with OT networks needs strong authentication between internal microservices after an attacker used a stolen API key from one service to query another. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. Which TWO responses best address the situation?

choices.text: Use mutual TLS with service-specific certificates issued by an internal CA.
Encode the API key with Base64 before storing it.
Place all services behind the same shared API key.
Allow any service in the subnet to call any other service.
Store private keys in a managed secret store or HSM-backed key management system.

questions.explanation: mTLS authenticates both parties and protects service-to-service communication. Managed key storage reduces key exposure. Shared keys and network trust alone are weaker.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A municipal government agency must verify that downloaded deployment artifacts were not modified while still allowing anyone to validate integrity. The service owner is concerned about user impact but accepts compensating controls when justified. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Encrypt the artifact with a shared password and email the password to all users.
Publish a cryptographic hash and a digital signature for each artifact.
Compress the artifact to reduce the chance of tampering.
Use reversible encoding so users can compare the encoded output.

questions.explanation: Hashes support integrity checks, while digital signatures bind the hash to a trusted signer. Encryption alone does not provide public integrity validation.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A logistics company is deploying database field-level encryption for sensitive records and wants to reduce the chance that application administrators can extract keys. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Use a centralized key management system with HSM-backed key protection and separation of duties.
Use one permanent key for all environments to simplify support.
Let database administrators export keys for troubleshooting.
Hard-code keys in the application repository with restricted branch access.

questions.explanation: A KMS backed by HSMs protects key material and supports rotation, audit, and separation of duties. Co-locating or hard-coding keys undermines encryption.

Executives want evidence that the control addresses root cause rather than only the visible symptom. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Rebuild from a minimal trusted base image, patch dependencies, and redeploy through the approved pipeline.
Run containers as root so patching is easier.
Expose the container runtime socket to the application.
Patch the running container manually and skip rebuilding the image.

questions.explanation: Containers should be rebuilt from patched, minimal images and deployed immutably. Image scanning and signing reduce supply chain and drift risk.

questions.stem: A multinational manufacturer finds a storage bucket containing customer exports readable by anyone with the URL. Rename the bucket to a random string.
Use a shorter URL expiration without changing public permissions.
Compress files to make them harder to inspect.

questions.explanation: Public cloud storage exposure is a misconfiguration. Access policies, logging review, and preventive guardrails address both exposure and recurrence.

choices.text: Use network segmentation, passive monitoring, strict allow-lists, and controlled jump-host access.
Run aggressive credentialed vulnerability scans weekly.
Install consumer antivirus on the controller.

questions.stem: A manufacturing company with OT networks detects that an administrative interface still accepts Telnet and legacy TLS ciphers on a management subnet. Update configuration baselines so drift from secure protocol standards is detected.
Disable insecure protocols and require SSH or TLS with approved cipher suites.
Keep Telnet but restrict it to a shorter password.
Use a private IP address as the only protection.

questions.explanation: Weak protocols expose credentials and sessions. Secure baselines should disable Telnet and legacy ciphers in favor of encrypted, approved protocols.

Trust URLs that resolve to private IP addresses.

Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Restore backups immediately onto the same network.
Isolate affected endpoints and disable compromised accounts before starting eradication and recovery.
Run a full vulnerability scan before containment.
Delete ransom notes as the first step.

questions.explanation: Containment limits spread before eradication and recovery. Restoring before containment can reintroduce encryption activity.

choices.text: Treat the pattern as password spraying and enforce MFA, lockout tuning, and detection for distributed attempts.
Classify the activity as brute force against one account.
Tell users to change passwords only after the next annual cycle.
Disable all account lockouts permanently.

questions.explanation: Password spraying uses low attempt counts across many accounts to avoid lockouts. MFA and spray-specific detection reduce risk.

Detect and rate-limit reused credential attempts, require step-up authentication, and check for account takeover indicators.
Block one country at the firewall and close the case.
Notify affected users and force password resets for accounts with suspicious successful logins.
Disable MFA because customers may be inconvenienced.

questions.explanation: Credential stuffing uses reused credentials from other breaches. Controls include rate limiting, anomaly detection, step-up authentication, and targeted resets.

Increase the recursive resolver cache size.
Allow the domain because DNS is required for business.
Investigate possible DNS tunneling and block or sinkhole the domain while preserving DNS logs.

Create a SOAR playbook that enriches indicators, opens a ticket, blocks confirmed indicators, and records approvals.

questions.stem: A managed service provider is responding to malware on several hosts and business leaders ask to wipe systems immediately. Maintain chain-of-custody records and create forensic images using approved tools.
Reimage the laptop before legal review.

questions.stem: A payment processor must demonstrate how personal data is collected, processed, retained, and deleted under applicable privacy obligations. Which TWO responses best address the situation?

choices.text: Treat privacy as only an encryption problem.
Maintain data inventories, processing records, retention schedules, and evidence of privacy control operation.
Align breach notification and data subject request procedures to legal requirements.
Keep personal data indefinitely because storage is cheap.
Store privacy decisions only in developer comments.

questions.explanation: Privacy compliance requires governance over data lifecycle and evidence, not just technical encryption.

questions.stem: A manufacturing company with OT networks wants to reduce compliance burden for payment processing systems. The service owner is concerned about user impact but accepts compensating controls when justified. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Share production card data with all developers.
Disable logging in payment systems.
Assume encryption alone removes all PCI obligations.
Segment cardholder data environments and use tokenization or validated payment services to reduce scope.

questions.explanation: Scope reduction uses segmentation, tokenization, and validated services, but controls must be tested and documented.

questions.stem: A municipal government agency is deciding whether to delay remediation of a control gap tied to regulatory reporting. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Delete evidence that shows missed control operation.
Assume no penalty exists unless a breach occurs.
Evaluate legal, financial, operational, and reputational consequences of noncompliance before accepting the delay.
Let the technical team accept regulatory risk alone.

questions.explanation: Compliance gaps can create legal and business consequences. Risk acceptance must come from accountable leadership with accurate evidence.

Use an external audit or independent assessment when third-party assurance is required.

choices.text: Assume no penalty exists unless a breach occurs.
Hide the gap from compliance reports.
Evaluate legal, financial, operational, and reputational consequences of noncompliance before accepting the delay.
Obtain formal risk acceptance from the appropriate authority if delay remains necessary.
Delete evidence that shows missed control operation.

questions.explanation: Compliance gaps can create legal and business consequences. Risk acceptance must come from accountable leadership with accurate evidence.

questions.stem: A payment processor wants independent assurance for customers but currently relies only on self-assessments by control owners. The service owner is concerned about user impact but accepts compensating controls when justified. The team has a small staff and needs a repeatable operational process. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Use an external audit or independent assessment when third-party assurance is required.
Ask control owners to attest anonymously.
Replace all internal monitoring with one annual external audit.
Use penetration testing as the only proof of governance.

questions.explanation: External audits provide independent assurance, while internal assessments support readiness and control improvement.

questions.stem: A manufacturing company with OT networks prepares for an audit and has screenshots without timestamps, owners, or linkage to control requirements. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Ask auditors to infer control operation from architecture diagrams only.
Collect evidence that is complete, dated, attributable, and mapped to specific control requirements.
Submit unlabeled screenshots because they show the screen.
Avoid retaining evidence to reduce discovery risk.

questions.explanation: Audit evidence must prove control design or operation for a defined period and be traceable to requirements.

questions.stem: A municipal government agency runs phishing simulations but only reports click rates without measuring reporting or credential submission. Executives want evidence that the control addresses root cause rather than only the visible symptom. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Tell users that all phishing emails are obvious.
Punish every user who clicks once.
Stop simulations after one successful campaign.
Track reporting rate, credential submission, repeat clickers, and time to report in addition to click rate.

questions.explanation: Awareness metrics should measure risky actions and positive behaviors, not only clicks.

questions.stem: A logistics company wants employees to report unusual coworker behavior and suspicious access requests without creating harassment or rumor channels. The implementation will become part of a standard operating procedure after validation. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. Which TWO responses best address the situation?

choices.text: Provide clear reporting channels, examples, and privacy-respecting escalation procedures.
Ask employees to publicly accuse coworkers in team chats.
Share all reports with the entire department.
Train staff to report observable behaviors and security concerns rather than personal judgments.
Discourage reporting unless proof is absolute.

questions.explanation: Awareness programs should make reporting easy, factual, and appropriately handled, while protecting privacy and due process.

questions.stem: A cloud-native startup needs developers, executives, and help desk staff to reduce different types of security risk. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The solution must avoid creating a new single point of failure. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Skip executive training because executives are too busy.
Give everyone the same generic annual slide deck only.
Train developers on physical badge policy instead of secure coding.
Use role-based training tailored to job duties, such as secure coding, executive phishing resistance, and help desk identity proofing.

questions.explanation: Role-based awareness is more effective because risks and decisions differ by function.

questions.stem: A legal services company has users receiving active phishing emails during an incident and needs to stop further credential submissions. The service owner is concerned about user impact but accepts compensating controls when justified. The team has a small staff and needs a repeatable operational process. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Ask users to investigate headers themselves.
Send a vague warning weeks later.
Tell users to delete all security emails.
Send clear, timely guidance with reporting instructions and examples of the active lure.

questions.explanation: Timely user guidance can reduce ongoing compromise and improve reporting during active campaigns.
